title: Wormia misorensis
intro: Wormia misorensis, also known as "Kutu," is a tropical plant species belonging to the Apocynaceae family. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, particularly in traditional medicine, where it is used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Wormia misorensis include alkaloids, flavonoids, and glycosides, which are responsible for its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. In herbal medicine, Wormia misorensis is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, and is traditionally used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues.
taxonomy: Wormia misorensis belongs to the family Orobanchaceae, which is a group of flowering plants commonly known as broomrapes or Indian paintbrushes. This family is a part of the order Lamiales, a large and diverse group of flowering plants. Within the family Orobanchaceae, Wormia misorensis is classified as a species of parasitic plant, relying on other plants for its nutritional needs. The plant is native to India, specifically in the states of Karnataka and Maharashtra. Wormia misorensis is often found in scrub and dry deciduous forests, where it grows as an epiphyte or semi-parasite.
